description: The provided image is unreadable and appears as a 'Check for updates' placeholder icon rather than a scientific figure. Based on the accompanying text extraction summary, this figure presumably displays characterization data for three arc-clad Fe-C-Cr-Mn-Si steel validation samples (Sample 1, Sample 2, and Sample 3). The figure likely contains X-ray diffraction (XRD) patterns identifying Sample 1 as single-phase Fe-Cr solid solution with FCC lattice (peaks 100, 200, 211) and Samples 2-3 as Fe-Cr-Ni FCC phases, with peak shifts indicating lattice distortion from atomic size mismatches. It may additionally present Vickers microhardness validation comparing experimental measurements (515 HV, 500 HV, 424 HV) against machine learning predictions (521 HV, 490 HV, 415 HV), demonstrating prediction errors of 1.17%, 2.00%, and 2.09%. These results support the paper's core claim that the CGAN-augmented SVR model with modified solid solution strengthening features achieves extreme high accuracy for steel property prediction.

The figure presents a machine learning feature importance analysis for Fe-C-Cr-Mn-Si steel design, divided into two conceptual panels. The left panel labeled "Data set" displays a grid of numerous small scatter plots or correlation matrices visualizing the training dataset (initially 173 points from MatWeb, augmented to 192 via CGANs), though individual plot details remain unreadable at this resolution. The right panel shows an "Importance ranking" funnel chart revealing the hierarchical influence of alloying elements on microhardness prediction. Chromium (Cr) is ranked at the top as the most significant element, followed by nickel (Ni) and molybdenum (Mo), with additional elements indicated by ellipsis at the bottom. This ranking derives from comprehensive feature engineering including Recursive Feature Elimination (RFE) voting, Pearson correlation analysis, and exhaustive screening combined with solid solution strengthening theory. The visualization supports the core conclusion that Cr, Ni, and Mo positively impact steel hardness, while elements like Mn, V, and W show weaker correlations, guiding the inverse design optimization to achieve predicted hardness up to 733.59 HV.

This figure presents comprehensive feature engineering analysis for machine learning prediction of Fe-C-Cr-Mn-Si steel microhardness. The first composite panel includes: (a) a heatmap displaying feature selection results for hardness prediction with color-coded matrices showing correlation patterns; (b) a Pearson correlation matrix using colored circles (blue indicating negative, red indicating positive correlations) between compositional and physical features; (c) a bar chart ranking feature importance, where shear modulus (G), difference in cohesive energy (δ_Ec), and local cohesive energy mismatch (D_Ec) emerge as dominant predictors; and (d) partially readable supplementary data. The second panel plots cross-validation scores against numbers of features, showing performance stabilization beyond three features, thereby validating the optimal feature subset selection. These visualizations demonstrate that solid solution strengthening theory-based parameters (G, δ_Ec, D_Ec) identified through recursive feature elimination and exhaustive screening are critical for accurate hardness prediction (R²=0.89), supporting the integration of physical metallurgy principles into the SVR model.

This figure presents a parity plot (scatter diagram) comparing predicted versus experimental solid solution strengthening stress (Δσ_SSS) values for Fe-C-Cr-Mn-Si steel samples using a Support Vector Machine (SVR) model. The circular data points represent 'All Samples' and are color-coded by prediction error magnitude (ranging from 0.60 MPa in dark blue to 29.90 MPa in dark red according to the vertical color bar). A dashed 'Guide line' indicates the ideal 1:1 correlation where predictions exactly match experimental values. The plot axes span 1200–1800 MPa for both experimental (x-axis) and predicted (y-axis) Δσ_SSS. Key model performance metrics are annotated directly on the figure: RMSE of 0.31 and R² of 0.89, indicating extreme high prediction accuracy. The tight clustering of data points along the diagonal guideline demonstrates that the SVR model, incorporating modified solid solution strengthening theory features (shear modulus G, cohesive energy difference δ_Ec, and local cohesive energy mismatch D_Ec), reliably predicts the strengthening contribution across the arc-cladded steel dataset, with the majority of predictions showing errors below 15 MPa.

This bar chart presents a comparative validation of machine learning predictions against experimental measurements for Vickers microhardness (HV) across three distinct Fe-C-Cr-Mn-Si steel cladding samples fabricated via arc cladding on ASTM A36 steel substrate. The x-axis labels three specimens (Sample 1, Sample 2, Sample 3) with varying compositions: Sample 1 contains 14 wt% Cr and 0.45 wt% C; Sample 2 contains 5.3 wt% Cr, 0.53 wt% C with additions of Ni (1.13%), W (1.19%), and V (0.25%); Sample 3 contains 5.2 wt% Cr, 0.15 wt% C with Ni (1.2%), Mo (1.5%), and V (0.25%). Blue bars denote predicted values generated by the support vector regression (SVR) model incorporating modified solid solution strengthening theory, while pink bars represent experimental microhardness values measured under 1 kg load with 20 s dwell time. Sample 1 shows predicted 521 HV versus experimental 515 HV (1.17% error); Sample 2 shows predicted 490 HV versus experimental 500 HV (2.00% error); Sample 3 shows predicted 415 HV versus experimental 424 HV (2.09% error). Error bars indicate measurement variability. The consistent agreement within 2.1% across all three compositions validates the extreme high accuracy of the machine learning pipeline combining conditional generative adversarial networks (CGANs) for data augmentation, feature engineering focusing on shear modulus and cohesive energy parameters, and Bayesian optimization, demonstrating reliable inverse design capability for high-performance steel coatings.

This figure presents a correlation scatter matrix (pair plot) visualizing multivariate relationships between eleven chemical composition variables and microhardness in Fe-C-Cr-Mn-Si steel alloys. The diagonal panels display probability density distributions (histograms with kernel density estimation curves) for each parameter: carbon (C), silicon (Si), manganese (Mn), chromium (Cr), sulfur (S), nickel (Ni), phosphorus (P), iron (Fe), molybdenum (Mo), vanadium (V), and tungsten (W) in weight percent, alongside Vickers microhardness (HV). The off-diagonal scatter plots reveal pairwise correlations between these features across the dataset. Notable trends include strong negative correlations between Fe content and other alloying elements (consistent with Fe being the matrix element), and positive correlations between microhardness and certain alloying elements such as Cr, Mo, W, and C, supporting solid solution strengthening effects. This correlation structure informs the feature engineering for machine learning models (SVR with R²=0.89) used to predict microhardness in the arc-cladded coating samples.

This figure presents a comprehensive feature selection workflow for machine learning-based microhardness prediction of Fe-C-Cr-Mn-Si steel fabricated by arc cladding. Panel (a) displays a heatmap of recursive feature elimination (RFE) voting results across three algorithms (XGBoost, RF, and LightGBM), where dark blue cells (ranking = 1) indicate significant features including EI-2nd, ηB, ω, δEI-2nd, DEc, δEc, γ, G, and e/a. Panel (b) shows the Pearson correlation coefficient (PCC) heatmap among these nine selected features, revealing six pairs with absolute PCC values ≥ 0.95 (e.g., EI-2nd vs ηB at -1.00, ω vs ηB at 0.98, γ vs DEc at -0.95), indicating high multicollinearity. Panel (c) presents the importance scores ranking, where EI-2nd exhibits the highest score (~0.46), followed by ηB (~0.14) and ω (~0.13). Based on this analysis, ηB, ω, δEI-2nd, and γ were removed due to lower importance scores within highly correlated pairs. The figure supports the core conclusion that e/a (valence electron concentration), G (shear modulus), EI-2nd (second ionization energy), δEc (cohesive energy difference), and DEc (local cohesive energy mismatch) serve as the definitive key features for accurately predicting microhardness through solid solution strengthening theory.

This scatter plot presents exhaustive screening results illustrating the relationship between feature number and cross-validation scores for machine learning prediction of Fe-C-Cr-Mn-Si steel microhardness. The x-axis shows the number of features ranging from 1 to 5, while the y-axis displays cross-validation scores spanning approximately 0.2 to 1.4. With only 1 feature, scores exhibit high variability (0.3–1.3), indicating unstable single-feature model performance. As features increase to 2, the distribution narrows to roughly 0.25–0.7. Critically, at 3 features, scores concentrate in a lower range (approximately 0.25–0.35), demonstrating optimal model stability. This aligns with the study's identification of three key parameters—shear modulus (G), difference in cohesive energy (δ_Ec), and local cohesive energy mismatch (D_Ec)—from modified solid solution strengthening theory. The trend confirms that 3 features constitute the optimal configuration for the support vector regression model, as adding more features (4–5) yields no performance improvement, validating the feature selection strategy for high-accuracy steel property prediction.

Figure 5 presents a comparative evaluation of nine machine learning models for predicting the microhardness of Fe-C-Cr-Mn-Si steel through scatter plots of predicted versus experimental values. Subfigures (a) through (i) correspond to Support Vector Regression (SVR), Extra Trees (ET), Multi-Layer Perceptron (MLP), Ridge Regression, LightGBM, XGBoost, Random Forest (RF), Linear Regression (LR), and Decision Tree (DT) models, respectively. Each plot displays training data (blue circles) and testing data (orange circles) against a diagonal dashed line representing perfect prediction within the 300-800 HV range. The SVR model (a) demonstrates superior performance with R² = 0.85 and RMSE = 0.39, while LightGBM (e) shows the poorest accuracy (R² = 0.63, RMSE = 0.62). Other models exhibit intermediate performance: Ridge (R²=0.82), MLP (R²=0.81), ET (R²=0.80), XGBoost (R²=0.79), LR (R²=0.76), RF (R²=0.74), and DT (R²=0.72). The models utilized three key input features—shear modulus G, cohesive energy difference δ_Ec, and local cohesive energy mismatch D_Ec—derived from solid solution strengthening theory. These results demonstrate that SVR achieves the highest prediction accuracy and generalization capability for microhardness prediction in this alloy system.

This figure presents a parity plot comparing machine learning predicted values versus experimental measurements of solid solution strengthening increment (Δσ_SSS) for Fe-C-Cr-Mn-Si steels. The scatter plot displays predicted Δσ_SSS (MPa) on the y-axis against experimental values (MPa) on the x-axis, with data points colored according to prediction error ranging from 0.60 to 29.90 MPa as indicated by the color bar. The plot includes a diagonal guide line representing perfect prediction (y=x). Statistical metrics are provided: RMSE of 0.31 and R² of 0.89, indicating high prediction accuracy. The model employs ten-fold cross-validation using three input features—difference in cohesive energy (δ_Ec), local cohesive energy mismatch (D), and shear modulus (G)—selected through feature engineering. The data points represent all samples including three experimentally validated cladding samples. The tight clustering around the guide line and high R² value demonstrate the effectiveness of the Support Vector Regression model combined with modified solid solution strengthening theory for predicting mechanical properties in multi-component steel systems.

This figure presents a vertical bar chart comparing the predicted microhardness values of inverse-designed Fe-C-Cr-Mn-Si steel obtained through four distinct intelligent optimization algorithms. The horizontal axis identifies the optimization methods: Firefly Algorithm (FA), Particle Swarm Optimization (PSO), Simulated Annealing (SA), and Grey Wolf Optimizer (GWO), while the vertical axis quantifies microhardness in Vickers Hardness (HV) units ranging from 0 to 800 HV. The specific values displayed atop each bar indicate that FA achieves the maximum predicted microhardness of 733.59 HV, followed closely by PSO at 730.04 HV, then GWO at 708.60 HV, and SA exhibiting the lowest value at 700.37 HV. This comparative visualization demonstrates the performance hierarchy of these heuristic algorithms in alloy design optimization, establishing FA as the most effective approach for maximizing microhardness in Fe-C-Cr-Mn-Si steel systems. The caption notes that FA represents a generalized form of SA, PSO and differential evolution with superior local attraction capacity, explaining its enhanced optimization capability for this materials design problem.

This figure presents X-ray diffraction (XRD) patterns of three arc-clad Fe-C-Cr-Mn-Si alloy cladding samples designed via machine learning. Panel (a) displays the full-range diffraction profiles spanning 2θ = 20-100°, where Sample 1 (Ni-free, 14 wt% Cr) exhibits a primary Fe-Cr solid solution phase with characteristic diffraction peaks indexed as (110), (200), and (211). In contrast, Samples 2 (1.13 wt% Ni, 5.3 wt% Cr) and 3 (1.2 wt% Ni, 5.2 wt% Cr) both display a Fe-Cr-Ni phase with identical peak indices, indicating a phase transition with increasing Ni content. Panel (b) provides an enlarged view of the (110) peak region between 40-50°, clearly showing that all alloy peaks are shifted to higher angles (rightward) relative to the standard Fe peak position (marked by vertical dashed line at approximately 44°). This systematic peak shift indicates lattice distortion caused by the atomic radius mismatch between Cr, Ni, and Fe atoms, providing experimental evidence for solid solution strengthening effects that validate the developed predictive model.

This bar chart validates the accuracy of machine learning predictions by comparing predicted versus experimental microhardness values for three distinct Fe-C-Cr-Mn-Si alloy samples produced via arc cladding. Blue bars denote machine learning predicted values, while reddish bars represent experimentally measured Vickers hardness (HV) values, with error bars indicating measurement variability. Sample 1 exhibits a predicted hardness of 521 HV against an experimental value of 515 HV (1.17% relative error), Sample 2 shows 490 HV predicted versus 500 HV experimental (2.00% error), and Sample 3 displays 415 HV predicted versus 424 HV experimental (2.09% error). The close agreement across all three validation samples, with prediction errors remaining below 2.1%, demonstrates the extreme high accuracy of the developed data-driven model incorporating modified solid solution strengthening theory for Fe-C-Cr-Mn-Si steel design.
